target.name: Twelve
target.founder: Nicholas Flanders, Etosha Cave, Kendra Kuhl

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series C → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.7x) to $472M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 3 same-sector + same-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $700M - $2.0B.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: KoBold Metals
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series C), post-money $2B+

private.name: Electric Hydrogen
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series C), post-money $1B+

private.name: Amogy
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series C), post-money $700M

data.narrative:
Twelve is a Series C-stage climate and energy company that has raised $472M to date. The valuation analysis draws from a comparable universe of three same-sector, same-stage private companies (KoBold Metals at $2.0B, Electric Hydrogen at $1.0B, and Amogy at $700M) alongside a broader peer set of eight private firms and one public comparable.

Two triangulation methods were applied. The stage-progression approach uses total capital raised as a proxy baseline and applies a 1.7x multiplier typical of Series C advancement, yielding $802M. The peer-median method references the three direct comparables, which have a median valuation of $1.0B and a range of $700M to $2.0B, directly implying $1.0B.

These methods support a triangulated valuation range of $682M to $1.1B, with a median of $901M. The wide range—spanning $418M between low and high—reflects material uncertainty in comparable valuations and the early-stage nature of climate-tech funding environments. Key risks to valuation include the sparseness of the peer set, stage-dependent volatility in private climate-tech multiples, and the absence of revenue or profitability milestones in the dataset. The peer-median method carries greater weight given direct sector and stage alignment, anchoring the upper portion of the range.
